input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building as a physical asset within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, characterized by its lifecycle stages from initial design to refurbishment, requiring integration of static models with runtime data for simulation, analysis, and optimization of infrastructure management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Real-time monitoring of infrastructure status, predictive analytics for maintenance and performance forecasting, and operational optimization services targeting AECO stakeholders (e.g., architects, engineers, facility managers) to reduce operational costs and enhance collaboration across the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, with runtime configuration management enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple digital twin instances are coordinated via a centralized DT Framework architecture, with distinct roles: Building Control Engineers handle device modeling and deployment initiation, Facility Managers manage real-time monitoring and control, and DT Engineers extend capabilities through service integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The modular architecture features standardized extension points for component integration, with the DT Framework acting as a central hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange and holistic building lifecycle management, supported by runtime configuration management for adaptive adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems is addressed through non-functional requirements, with the DT Framework facilitating bidirectional data exchange and integration with BIM tools for device modeling, supporting scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs geometric BIM models enriched with semantic data for Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers), where behavioral models are defined via control logic by Building Control Engineers. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) is used to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models, automatically generating deployable DT artifacts. Real-time data exchange ensures synchronization between physical infrastructure and the digital twin through standardized interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control, enabling Facility Managers to interact with the deployed Digital Twin for real-time monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT Framework acts as a central hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange through a modular architecture with standardized extension points for service integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Model-driven deployment leveraging Unreal Engine for interactive visualization, standardized extension points for modular service integration, and runtime configuration management enabling dynamic adjustments without full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial device modeling within BIM for sensors, actuators, and controllers; Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) based transformation to structured representations; automated generation of deployable DT artifacts; real-time data synchronization between physical and digital twins; interactive visualization for monitoring and control; and modular evolution through standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process employs Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to automate the transformation of enriched BIM models into structured DT representations and deployable artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is supported through a modular architecture featuring standardized extension points, enabling DT Engineers to integrate custom services and analytics for adapting to operational changes.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision-making is facilitated through immersive interactive visualization interfaces used by Facility Managers to monitor system states and operate physical infrastructure. Custom analytics services integrated via standardized extension points provide operational insights, though specific methodologies and confidence measures are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework utilizes Building Information Modeling (BIM) standards for initial device modeling and incorporates standardized extension points to ensure interoperability and integration of custom services, addressing the industry's lack of standardized digital twin integration frameworks.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The extraction correctly identifies the coordination roles and the centralized nature of the framework as described in the text. It cites the 'DT Framework acts as a central hub' and aligns with Table I, which defines distinct roles: 'Building Control Engineer', 'Facility Manager', and 'DT Engineer' with the specific responsibilities listed in the extraction.

The extraction provides a comprehensive summary of the system's orchestration, supported by multiple sections of the text. It aligns with the contribution 'R4: A modular architecture with defined extension points' and the Solution Proposal which states: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.' It also correctly includes the runtime configuration aspect for adaptability.

The extraction is well-supported by the section on non-functional requirements and the system architecture. The text mentions 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' and 'scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems' as key non-functional requirements. It also describes the framework as 'facilitating bidirectional data exchange' and being 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)' for device modeling.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately synthesizes the core information from the source text. It correctly identifies the use of BIM models enriched with semantic data for BCS components, which is supported by the text stating: 'embedded domain-specific control semantics for lighting systems directly into BIM models' and 'embedding domain-specific control semantics... transforming them from static planning artifacts into dynamic, runtime-capable digital representations'. It accurately attributes the definition of control logic to Building Control Engineers, which is supported by the text: 'Building Control Engineers who model devices and implement control logic'. The description of using MDE to extract representations and generate artifacts is directly supported: 'Our approach automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces' and 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts'. Finally, the mention of real-time synchronization is supported by: 'supports real-time data exchange' and 'Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the document mentions 'actuators' in the context of modeling ('define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'), it does not provide specific details about the physical acting components themselves. The characteristic description requires information on types of actuators, control capabilities, remote interfaces, ranges, limitations, safety constraints, and addressing mechanisms. The source text focuses on the software framework and modeling aspects rather than the hardware specifications of the actuators, making 'Not in Document' the correct assessment for the specific details required.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions sensors and data (e.g., 'post-occupancy sensor data', 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers'), but it lacks the specific technical details required by the characteristic description. There is no information regarding specific sensor types, measurement capabilities, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, data transmission rates, accuracy/precision specifications, or detailed data collection mechanisms. The text focuses on the integration framework rather than the specifications of the sensing hardware, thus 'Not in Document' is appropriate.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions the use of the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' to evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT in the case study. However, it does not discuss general model fidelity requirements, broad validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources, error detection and correction mechanisms, or general quality assurance processes for models and data beyond this specific metric. Since the characteristic asks for a broad description of fidelity and validity considerations which are largely absent, 'Not in Document' is a correct assessment.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
